title: Beginner's Guide: Setup a Kubernetes Cluster
content: # Intro {#intro} In this post, I'll walk you through the steps needed to install K3s, a lightweight Kubernetes distribution. For this tutorial, I'm setting it up on an Atomic Pi, a low-cost and low-power single-board computer, but you can use any similar device like a small PC or a Raspberry Pi. Whether you're a developer looking to test a new application or a system administrator wanting to learn more about Kubernetes, this guide will provide the essential information to get you started. ### **Hardware I Recommend** {#hardware-i-recommend} Below are some Amazon links to hardware I’ve personally used and recommend. These mini PCs are affordable and energy-efficient. The Intel machines have hardware transcoding, really good if you want to run a plex container. ![Raspberry Pi 4 Model B Image](/images/2025/01/image-11.png) **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** - CPU: Quad-core Cortex-A72 (ARM v8) 64-bit SoC @ 1.5GHz - RAM: 2GB, 4GB, or 8GB - Storage: MicroSD Card Slot - Features: Dual 4K HDMI, Low Power, Broad OS Support - Price: $61.99 [Buy on Amazon](https://amzn.to/4aIQwCo) ![Beelink EQ14 Image](/images/2025/01/image-8.png) **Beelink EQ14** - CPU: Intel N150 up to 3.6GHz - RAM: 16GB - Storage: 500GB SSD - Features: Dual LAN, Hardware Transcoding, Low Power - Price: $189.00 (on sale) [Buy on Amazon](https://amzn.to/42kbyVv) ![Beelink EQ12 Image](/images/2025/01/image-9.png) **Beelink EQ12** - CPU: Intel Core 1220P (up to 4.4GHz) - RAM: 24GB - Storage: 500GB SSD - Features: Dual LAN - Price: $259.00 [Buy on Amazon](https://amzn.to/4hgM6Fk) ![Beelink Mini S12 Pro Image](/images/2025/01/image-10.png) **Beelink Mini S12 Pro** - CPU: N100 (4C/4T, Up to 3.4GHz) - RAM: 16GB - Storage: 500GB SSD - Features: Energy Efficient - Price: $159.00 (on sale) [Buy on Amazon](https://amzn.to/3WpMSXU) ## Ubuntu {#ubuntu} First, you'll need to download Ubuntu Server from the official website: [Ubuntu Server Download](https://ubuntu.com/download/server). As of 2026, **24.04 LTS** is the current long-term release — the steps below apply unchanged. ### Create a Bootable USB (Windows) {#create-a-bootable-usb-windows} To create a bootable USB drive on Windows, I recommend using [Rufus](https://rufus.ie/en/). 1. Open Rufus and select your flash drive from the "Device" drop-down menu. 2. Insert your USB stick. 3. In the "Boot selection" section, click the "Select" button and choose the Ubuntu ISO file you downloaded. 4. Click the "Start" button to begin creating the bootable flash drive. ![fd04f13dd3e608cb1328684aeafae066111c9c13_2_517x580](/images/2023/01/fd04f13dd3e608cb1328684aeafae066111c9c13_2_517x580.png) For more detailed instructions, check out this [article](https://ubuntu.com/tutorials/create-a-usb-stick-on-windows#1-overview). ### Install Ubuntu on Your System {#install-ubuntu-on-your-system} 1. Insert the flash drive into the system and power it on. 2. Press the "Del" key to enter the BIOS settings. (Atomic Pi specific) 3. Change the boot order to prioritize the flash drive. 4. Save the changes and exit the BIOS. 5. The system will boot from the flash drive and start the Ubuntu installer. 6. Follow the on-screen instructions to complete the installation process. 7. Ensure SSH is enabled for remote access. **Note:** It's recommended to back up any important data on the Your system before installing Ubuntu, as the installation process will erase all existing data. ## Install Nodes {#install-nodes} ### Master Node Setup {#master-node-setup} #### Connect Using SSH {#connect-using-ssh} Connect to your master node: ```bash ssh pi@192.168.1.100 ``` #### Configure Environment Variables {#configure-environment-variables} Disable servicelib and traefik since we will use MetalLB for load balancing. ```bash export K3S_KUBECONFIG_MODE="644" export INSTALL_K3S_EXEC="--disable servicelb --disable traefik" ``` #### Install K3s {#install-k3s} Run the following command to install K3s: ```bash curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh - ``` #### Uninstall (if needed) {#uninstall-if-needed} To uninstall K3s, use the script below: ```bash /usr/local/bin/k3s-uninstall.sh ``` #### Check the Status {#check-the-status} Verify that K3s is running: ```bash sudo systemctl status k3s ``` #### Get the Access Token {#get-the-access-token} Workers need an auth token to join the cluster. Retrieve it with this command: ```bash sudo cat /var/lib/rancher/k3s/server/node-token ``` ### Worker Node Setup {#worker-node-setup} #### Configure Environment Variables {#configure-environment-variables-1} ```bash export K3S_KUBECONFIG_MODE="644" export K3S_URL="https://192.168.1.101:6443" export K3S_TOKEN="Put the Master node token here!!" ``` #### Install K3s {#install-k3s-1} Install K3s on the worker node: ```bash curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh - ``` #### Uninstall (if needed) {#uninstall-if-needed-1} To uninstall K3s on the worker node, use the script below: ```bash /usr/local/bin/k3s-agent-uninstall.sh ``` ## Checking the Cluster {#checking-the-cluster} Use the following command to get the status of all resources in the cluster: ```bash kubectl get all -o wide ``` For more kubectl commands, refer to the [Kubernetes documentation](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/). ## Setup Local Workstation Kubectl {#setup-local-workstation-kubectl} I find it easier to apply manifests locally rather than through SSH. Here are the steps to control your cluster from your local machine. ### Get the Tools {#get-the-tools} Download the necessary tools from the [Kubernetes website](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/). ### Copy Configuration {#copy-configuration} Copy the kube config from your master node to your local machine: ```bash scp pi@192.168.1.100:/etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml ~/.kube/config ``` ### Replace the IP {#replace-the-ip} Open the configuration file (`~/.kube/config` or `C:\users\myuser\.kube\config`) and replace `127.0.0.1` with your master node's IP address (`192.168.1.100`). After this, you should be able to run kubectl commands from your local machine. Verify with: ```bash kubectl get all -o wide ``` ## Conclusion {#conclusion} Now that your cluster is set up, check out my Uptime Kuma Article to test out your new k3s cluster:
